Of Spyridon, Bishop of the Cypriots.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of Spyridon, Bishop of the Cypriots.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: With respect to Spyridon, so great was his sanctity while a shepherd, that he was thought worthy of being made a Pastor of men: and having been assigned the bishopric of one of the cities in Cyprus named Trimithus, on account of his extreme humility he continued to feed his sheep during his incumbency of the bishopric.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many extraordinary things are related of him: I shall however record but one or two, lest I should seem to wander from my subject.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Once about midnight, thieves having clandestinely entered his sheepfold attempted to carry off some of the sheep.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But God who protected the shepherd preserved his sheep also; for the thieves were by an invisible power bound to the folds.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At daybreak, when he came to the sheep and found the men with their hands tied behind them, he understood what was done: and after having prayed he liberated the thieves, earnestly admonishing and exhorting them to support themselves by honest labor, and not to take anything unjustly.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He then gave them a ram, and sent them away, humorously adding, ‘that ye may not appear to have watched all night in vain.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This is one of the miracles in connection with Spyridon.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He had a virgin daughter named Irene, who was a partaker of her father’s piety.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: An acquaintance entrusted to her keeping an ornament of considerable value: she, to guard it more securely, hid what had been deposited with her in the ground, and not long afterwards died.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Subsequently the owner of the property came to claim it; and not finding the virgin, he began an excited conversation with the father, at times accusing him of an attempt to defraud him, and then again beseeching him to restore the deposit.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The old man, regarding this person’s loss as his own misfortune, went to the tomb of his daughter, and called upon God to show him before its proper season the promised resurrection.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Nor was he disappointed in his hope: for the virgin again reviving appeared to her father, and having pointed out to him the spot where she had hidden the ornament, she once more departed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such characters as these adorned the churches in the time of the emperor Constantine.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These details I obtained from many inhabitants of Cyprus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: I have also found a treatise composed in Latin by the presbyter Rufinus, from which I have collected these and some other things which will be hereafter adduced.
